answer:
**Charles Comte's strongest formulation** appears in *Traité de la propriété* (Vol. I, ch. 1), where he argues that modern legal and moral ideas derive from ancient slaveholding societies (Greeks, Romans, and medievals), whose jurisprudence treated humans as property and thus contradicted natural rights/duties, preventing a true foundation for property in labor and distorting all subsequent systems.[^1] He illustrates this by noting how slave-owning peoples could not admit inherent human duties (e.g., familial) without undermining slavery, so their laws rested on governmental acts rather than nature, influencing us still: "we steep ourselves in the doctrines of domestic slavery... we invoke them in our courts of justice."[^2]

**Charles Dunoyer's strongest formulation** is in *Nouveau traité d'économie* (Vol. I, ch. 7), where he contends that reliance on slave labor inherently degrades masters' industry, intelligence, and morals—keeping them warlike, idle, and tyrannical—while stifling societal progress: "wherever men can compel others to work for them, it is very rare that they instruct themselves, that they become industrious"; slavery yields "ignorance, incapacity, softness, ostentation, iniquity, violence."[^3] He contrasts this with free industry as the path to true liberty.[^4]

These are **two different objections**, not the same case. Comte targets slavery's *intellectual legacy*: ancient jurisprudence's false foundations (property as state-granted, not labor-based) persist, warping modern law.[^5] Dunoyer targets slavery's *practical effects*: it blocks industrial/moral development in real time, dooming slave societies to barbarism.[^6] They share a surface anti-slavery thrust (both deem it unnatural/anti-progress), but Comte critiques enduring *doctrinal contamination* of legal systems, while Dunoyer critiques ongoing *economic degradation* of industry/society. The corpus yields no direct overlap or cross-reference resolving them as identical.[^7]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch. 1, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/01-chapitre-premier-de-linfluence-des-doctrines-des-p/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, ch. 1, [¶ p-e0cba9](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/01-chapitre-premier-de-linfluence-des-doctrines-des-p/translation/#p-e0cba9).

[^3]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch. 7, [¶ p-e73720](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/#p-e73720).

[^4]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch. 7, [translation](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/).

[^5]: Ibid., ch. 1, [¶ p-9641a8](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/01-chapitre-premier-de-linfluence-des-doctrines-des-p/translation/#p-9641a8).

[^6]: Ibid., ch. 7, [¶ p-312d6f](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/#p-312d6f).

[^7]: Note Dunoyer's footnote in ch. 7 explicitly contrasts his slavery view with Comte's (on origins), confirming divergence: "M. Comte would seem not to adopt this idea."[^8]

[^8]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, ch. 7, [¶ p-52489c](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/#p-52489c).
